title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Sensitivity to Light and Sound
meta_description: Is your spiritual awakening making you sensitive to light and sound? You are not broken. Learn why your senses are heightened and how to cope.
og_title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Sensitivity to Light and Sound
og_description: Is your spiritual awakening making you sensitive to light and sound? You are not broken. Learn why your senses are heightened and how to cope.
body:
You’re Not Broken - Your Senses Just Got Turned Up

Let’s start with something honest. If you’re reading this, there’s a good chance you’ve been told - directly or indirectly - that you’re too much. Too sensitive. Too reactive. That you need to “toughen up” or “stop being so dramatic” when the fluorescent lights at the grocery store feel like a personal attack on your nervous system, or when the sound of someone chewing sends you into a spiral of irritation.

Here’s the thing: nothing is wrong with you. You’re not broken. Your nervous system isn’t defective. It’s actually working exactly as it should - for someone who’s waking up to a wider range of reality than most people ever experience.

Spiritual awakening doesn’t just change your beliefs or your worldview. It physically rewires your brain and your body. And one of the most common - and least talked about - side effects is a sudden, sometimes overwhelming sensitivity to light and sound. You might feel like you’ve been living in a dim, quiet room your whole life, and someone just threw open the curtains and cranked up the volume on the whole world. Right?!

I’m Kalesh, and I’ve been where you are. I’ve felt that raw, exposed, “I can’t be in this room for one more second” feeling. So let’s talk about what’s really going on. Let’s talk about why spiritual awakening turns up the volume on your senses, and - more importantly - what you can do about it.

“Your sensitivity isn’t a flaw. It’s the price of admission to a life where you feel everything - and that includes the pain of waking up to a world you were never taught to see.”

What We Mean by “Spiritual Awakening” - And Why It Changes Your Body

Before we get into the light and sound stuff, we need to be clear about what spiritual awakening actually is. It’s not a weekend retreat or a cool new belief system. It’s a fundamental shift in how you perceive reality. It’s the moment - or the slow process - where the veil between your ordinary awareness and something much bigger starts to thin.

For some people, it happens like a lightning strike. A sudden loss, a near-death experience, a psychedelic journey. For others, it creeps in slowly - a quiet knowing that the world you were told was “real” isn’t the whole story. Either way, the result is the same: you start to perceive energy, patterns, and information that most people can’t see or feel.

And here’s where it gets physical. Your nervous system is designed to filter out most of the sensory input coming at you at any given moment. That’s called sensory gating. It’s what lets you focus on a conversation in a noisy café or ignore the hum of your refrigerator. But when you’re going through a spiritual awakening, that filter starts to loosen. Sometimes it falls apart completely.

You start to pick up on things you couldn’t before. The subtle flicker of a light bulb. The high-pitched whine of electronics. The emotional weight in someone’s voice. The energy of a room when you walk in. And that’s beautiful - but it’s also exhausting. Your body wasn’t built to process this much input without some serious adjustment.

Does that land? Because if you’ve been feeling like you’re going crazy, I want you to hear this: you’re not. You’re just wired for more. And your nervous system is doing its best to keep up.

The Biology of Sensitivity - What’s Happening in Your Brain

Let’s get a little nerdy for a minute. Your brain has a region called the thalamus. Think of it as the switchboard operator for your senses. Every sight, sound, touch, and taste gets routed through the thalamus before it reaches the parts of your brain that interpret it. In most people, the thalamus does a good job of filtering out irrelevant noise.

But in people who are highly sensitive - and especially in people going through a spiritual awakening - that filter is thinner. Research on sensory processing sensitivity (SPS) shows that sensitive people have more activity in the brain regions responsible for processing sensory information. They literally take in more data from their environment. That’s why a single loud noise can feel like a physical blow, or why a bright room can trigger a headache or anxiety.

Now add spiritual awakening to the mix. When your consciousness expands, your brain starts to rewire itself. This is called neuroplasticity. You’re literally building new neural pathways to accommodate the new information you’re receiving. And during that process, your sensory system goes into overdrive. It’s like your brain is saying, “I don’t know what’s important yet, so I’m going to pay attention to EVERYTHING.”

That’s why you might find yourself suddenly unable to tolerate bright lights, loud noises, or even the sound of your own thoughts. Your nervous system is in a state of high alert. It’s not a sign that something is wrong. It’s a sign that you’re in the middle of a major upgrade.

Why Light Hurts - The “Bright Wound”

Light sensitivity is one of the most common complaints I hear from people in the middle of an awakening. And it makes sense. Light is information. It carries frequency, vibration, and in many spiritual traditions, it’s considered the very fabric of consciousness itself. When you’re waking up, you’re becoming more sensitive to that fabric.

But here’s the practical side: your eyes have photoreceptors that are directly connected to your pineal gland - the so-called “third eye” that’s associated with spiritual insight and intuition. When your pineal gland starts to activate or decalcify, it can make your eyes more sensitive to light. It’s not a metaphor. It’s biology.

You might notice that natural sunlight feels different. It might feel nourishing one day and overwhelming the next. Fluorescent lighting? Forget it. It can feel like being scraped from the inside out. That’s because fluorescent lights flicker at a frequency that most people don’t consciously perceive, but your newly sensitive nervous system picks it up loud and clear. It’s like being trapped in a strobe light all day.

If this sounds familiar, you’re not alone. Many people report needing sunglasses indoors, avoiding bright stores, or feeling a physical aversion to screens during their awakening. The good news is that this sensitivity usually stabilizes over time, as your nervous system learns to integrate the new input. But in the meantime, you need practical tools to cope.

One thing that helps is giving your eyes a break. Literally. Spend time in dimmer environments. Use red or amber light bulbs at night - they don’t stimulate your pineal gland the way blue light does. And if you’re looking for a resource that explains the connection between light, the pineal gland, and spiritual awakening in more depth, check out this book: See on Amazon (paid link). It goes deep into the science and the spirituality of what’s happening in your brain.

Why Sound Becomes Unbearable - The Noise of a Wider World

Sound sensitivity is the other side of the coin. And honestly, it can be even harder to manage because you can’t just close your ears the way you can close your eyes.

During a spiritual awakening, your auditory system becomes more sensitive to frequency. You might start hearing things you never noticed before - the hum of a refrigerator from two rooms away, the ticking of a clock that’s been there for years, the way someone’s voice carries a hidden emotion they’re not expressing. You might even start hearing high-pitched tones or ringing in your ears. That’s called tinnitus, and it’s incredibly common during awakening. Some traditions say it’s the sound of your energy field recalibrating. Others say it’s you tuning into higher frequencies.

Whatever the cause, it’s real. And it can make everyday life feel like an assault. Crowded restaurants, open-plan offices, even a conversation with a loud friend can leave you feeling drained, irritable, or physically sick.

Here’s what I want you to know: your sensitivity to sound is not a weakness. It’s a sign that you’re perceiving more of the information that’s always been there. You’re not making it up. You’re just hearing what others have learned to ignore. And that’s a gift - but it’s a gift that comes with a steep learning curve.

You need to protect your ears the way you’d protect your eyes. Invest in good noise-canceling headphones. Give yourself quiet time every day. And if you’re in a situation where you can’t escape the noise, try grounding techniques - pressing your feet into the floor, taking slow breaths, or focusing on a single point of sensation in your body. It won’t make the noise go away, but it will help your nervous system stay regulated.

If you want to understand more about how sound and frequency affect your nervous system, this book is a great place to start: See on Amazon (paid link). It’s written for people who are going through exactly what you’re going through.

Your Nervous System Is Learning a New Language

Think of your nervous system as a muscle. Right now, it’s in the middle of a workout. It’s being stretched, challenged, and reshaped. And like any muscle, it’s going to be sore and sensitive while it adapts.

That’s the core insight you need to hold onto. Your sensitivity to light and sound isn’t permanent. It’s a phase. It’s the training wheels of a wider perception. As your nervous system learns to integrate the new information, the intensity will start to settle. You’ll learn to filter again - but this time, you’ll be in control of the filter.

But here’s the catch: you can’t rush it. And you can’t force your nervous system to go back to the way it was. The old filter is gone. You can’t unsee what you’ve seen, or unhear what you’ve heard. The only way through is forward.

So be patient with yourself. If you need to leave a party early because the music is too loud, leave. If you need to wear sunglasses on a cloudy day, wear them. If you need to sit in silence for an hour after a conversation, do it. Your nervous system is telling you what it needs. Trust it.

Practical Tools to Manage Light and Sound Sensitivity

Let’s get concrete. Here are some things that actually help when you’re in the thick of it.

  • Create a sensory-safe space. Have at least one room in your home where you control the lighting and the sound. Dim lamps, blackout curtains, and soft textures make a huge difference.
  • Use earplugs or noise-canceling headphones. Carry them with you everywhere. They’re not a crutch - they’re a tool. You wouldn’t run a marathon without proper shoes. Don’t run your day without proper ear protection.
  • Limit screen time, especially blue light. Blue light from phones and computers can overstimulate your pineal gland and make light sensitivity worse. Use blue-light-blocking glasses or install a blue-light filter on your devices.
  • Practice earthing or grounding. Walk barefoot on grass, dirt, or sand. It helps discharge the excess electrical energy that can build up in a sensitive nervous system.
  • Hydrate and eat grounding foods. Root vegetables, bone broth, and warm soups help stabilize your energy. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar, which can amp up your sensitivity even more.
  • Give yourself permission to rest. Your nervous system is working overtime. It needs more sleep, more quiet, and more downtime than it used to. Honor that.

One book that helped me understand the nervous system’s role in spiritual awakening is this one: See on Amazon (paid link). It talks about how trauma and awakening intersect, and why your body reacts the way it does.

The Gift Underneath the Discomfort

I know it doesn’t feel like a gift right now. When you’re lying in a dark room because the sunlight hurts, or when you’re avoiding your favorite café because the espresso machine is too loud, it’s hard to see the upside. But I promise you, it’s there.

Your sensitivity is not a punishment. It’s a refinement. It’s your system becoming more precise, more attuned, more capable of perceiving the subtle energies that most people walk right past. The same sensitivity that makes you wince at a loud noise is the sensitivity that will one day let you feel the energy of a room, sense when someone is lying, or hear the whispered guidance of your own intuition.

You’re not losing your edge. You’re sharpening it. And that process is uncomfortable. It’s supposed to be. But on the other side of this adjustment period is a version of you that can hold space for the full spectrum of human experience - the bright and the dark, the loud and the silent.

Right now, you’re in the chrysalis. It’s tight, it’s dark, and it’s quiet. But the wings are forming. Give them time.

When to Seek Help

One last thing. While sensitivity to light and sound is a normal part of spiritual awakening, it can also overlap with physical conditions like migraines, vestibular disorders, or sensory processing issues. If your sensitivity is so severe that it’s preventing you from functioning - if you can’t leave the house, if you’re in constant pain, if you’re losing sleep for weeks on end - please see a doctor or a neurologist. There’s no shame in getting medical support. Spiritual awakening and physical health are not separate. You need both.

This book is a great resource for understanding the intersection of spirituality and mental health: See on Amazon (paid link). It’s written by a psychiatrist who works with people going through spiritual emergencies, and it’s full of practical wisdom.

You’re not alone in this. You’re not broken. And you’re not too sensitive. You’re exactly where you need to be - right in the middle of a transformation that will change everything.

If you want to go deeper into understanding your sensitivity and how to work with it rather than against it, I’ve put together a free resource that walks you through the first steps of integrating your awakening. Take the free assessment here to see where you are on the journey and get personalized guidance for your next steps.
